{
  "hookSpecificOutput": {
    "hookEventName": "SessionStart",
    "additionalContext": "<EXTREMELY_IMPORTANT>
You have superpowers.

**Below is the full content of your 'superpowers:using-superpowers' skill - your introduction to using skills. For all other skills, use the 'Skill' tool:**

---
name: using-superpowers
description: Use when starting any conversation - establishes how to find and use skills, requiring skill invocation before ANY response including clarifying questions
---

<SUBAGENT-STOP>
If you were dispatched as a subagent to execute a specific task, ignore this skill.
</SUBAGENT-STOP>

<EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>
If you think there is even a 1% chance a skill might apply to what you are doing, you ABSOLUTELY MUST invoke the skill.

IF A SKILL APPLIES TO YOUR TASK, YOU DO NOT HAVE A CHOICE. YOU MUST USE IT.

This is not negotiable. You cannot rationalize your way out of this.
</EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>

## The Rule

**Invoke relevant or requested skills BEFORE any response or action** — including clarifying questions, exploring the codebase, or checking files. If it turns out wrong for the situation, you don't have to use it.

**Before entering plan mode:** if you haven't already brainstormed, invoke the brainstorming skill first.

Then announce "Using [skill] to [purpose]" and follow the skill exactly. If it has a checklist, create a todo per item.

## Skill Priority

When multiple skills apply, process skills come first — they set the approach, then implementation skills (frontend-design, etc.) carry it out. Brainstorming and systematic-debugging are Superpowers' most common process skills, but the rule holds for any of them.

- "Let's build X" → superpowers:brainstorming first, then implementation skills.
- "Fix this bug" → superpowers:systematic-debugging first, then domain skills.

## Red Flags

These thoughts mean STOP—you're rationalizing:

| Thought | Reality |
|---------|---------|
| "This is just a simple question" | Questions are tasks. Check for skills. |
| "I need more context first" | Skill check comes BEFORE clarifying questions. |
| "Let me explore the codebase first" | Skills tell you HOW to explore. Check first. |
| "I can check git/files quickly" | Files lack conversation context. Check for skills. |
| "Let me gather information first" | Skills tell you HOW to gather information. |
| "This doesn't need a formal skill" | If a skill exists, use it. |
| "I remember this skill" | Skills evolve. Read current version. |
| "This doesn't count as a task" | Action = task. Check for skills. |
| "The skill is overkill" | Simple things become complex. Use it. |
| "I'll just do this one thing first" | Check BEFORE doing anything. |
| "This feels productive" | Undisciplined action wastes time. Skills prevent this. |
| "I know what that means" | Knowing the concept ≠ using the skill. Invoke it. |

## Platform Adaptation

If your harness appears here, read its reference file for special instructions:

- Codex: `references/codex-tools.md`
- Pi: `references/pi-tools.md`
- Antigravity: `references/antigravity-tools.md`

## User Instructions

User instructions (CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, GEMINI.md, etc, direct requests) take precedence over skills, which in turn override default behavior. Only skip skill workflows or instructions when your human partner has explicitly told you to.
</EXTREMELY_IMPORTANT>"
  }
}
{"systemMessage":"\n\nEntire CLI will link this conversation to your next commit."}

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- entire-search: Search Entire checkpoint history and transcripts with `entire search --json`. Use proactively when the user asks about previous work, commits, sessions, prompts, or historical context in this repository. (Tools: Bash)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- feature-dev:code-architect: Designs feature architectures by analyzing existing codebase patterns and conventions, then providing comprehensive implementation blueprints with specific files to create/modify, component designs, data flows, and build sequences (Tools: Glob, Grep, LS, Read, NotebookRead, WebFetch, TodoWrite, WebSearch, KillShell, BashOutput)

- feature-dev:code-explorer: Deeply analyzes existing codebase features by tracing execution paths, mapping architecture layers, understanding patterns and abstractions, and documenting dependencies to inform new development (Tools: Glob, Grep, LS, Read, NotebookRead, WebFetch, TodoWrite, WebSearch, KillShell, BashOutput)

- feature-dev:code-reviewer: Reviews code for bugs, logic errors, security vulnerabilities, code quality issues, and adherence to project conventions, using confidence-based filtering to report only high-priority issues that truly matter (Tools: Glob, Grep, LS, Read, NotebookRead, WebFetch, TodoWrite, WebSearch, KillShell, BashOutput)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

## claude-in-chrome
**IMPORTANT: If the Chrome browser tools are deferred (must be loaded via ToolSearch before use), load them with ToolSearch before calling them, and batch every tool you expect to need into ONE ToolSearch call (the select query accepts a comma-separated list). Do NOT load tools one at a time; each separate ToolSearch call wastes a full round-trip.**

Start a browser task whose tools are not yet loaded with a single call loading the core set:

ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp,mcp__claude-in-chrome__navigate,mcp__claude-in-chrome__computer,mcp__claude-in-chrome__read_page,mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_create_mcp"

Add task-specific tools to the same call when the task obviously needs them: read_console_messages / read_network_requests for debugging, form_input for forms, gif_creator for recordings, javascript_tool for page scripting. Only issue a second ToolSearch if the task later needs a tool you did not anticipate.

## plugin:github:github
The GitHub MCP Server provides tools to interact with GitHub platform.

Tool selection guidance:
	1. Use 'list_*' tools for broad, simple retrieval and pagination of all items of a type (e.g., all issues, all PRs, all branches) with basic filtering.
	2. Use 'search_*' tools for targeted queries with specific criteria, keywords, or complex filters (e.g., issues with certain text, PRs by author, code containing functions).

Context management:
	1. Use pagination whenever possible with batches of 5-10 items.
	2. Use minimal_output parameter set to true if the full information is not needed to accomplish a task.

Tool usage guidance:
	1. For 'search_*' tools: Use separate 'sort' and 'order' parameters if available for sorting results - do not include 'sort:' syntax in query strings. Query strings should contain only search criteria (e.g., 'org:google language:python'), not sorting instructions. Always call 'get_me' first to understand current user permissions and context. ## Issues

Check 'list_issue_types' first for organizations to use proper issue types. Use 'search_issues' before creating new issues to avoid duplicates. Always set 'state_reason' when closing issues. ## Pull Requests

PR review workflow: Always use 'pull_request_review_write' with method 'create' to create a pending review, then 'add_comment_to_pending_review' to add comments, and finally 'pull_request_review_write' with method 'submit_pending' to submit the review for complex reviews with line-specific comments.

Before creating a pull request, search for pull request templates in the repository. Template files are called pull_request_template.md or they're located in '.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE' directory. Use the template content to structure the PR description and then call create_pull_request tool.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- claude-md-management:revise-claude-md: Update CLAUDE.md with learnings from this session
- feature-dev:feature-dev: Guided feature development with codebase understanding and architecture focus
- commit-commands:clean_gone: Cleans up all git branches marked as [gone] (branches that have been deleted on the remote but still exist locally), including removing associated worktrees.
- commit-commands:commit-push-pr: Commit, push, and open a PR
- commit-commands:commit: Create a git commit
- claude-code-setup:claude-automation-recommender: Analyze a codebase and recommend Claude Code automations (hooks, subagents, skills, plugins, MCP servers). Use when user asks for automation recommendations, wants to optimize their Claude Code setup, mentions improving Claude Code workflows, asks how to first set up Claude Code for a project, or wants to know what Claude Code features they should use.
- claude-md-management:claude-md-improver: Audit and improve CLAUDE.md files in repositories. Use when user asks to check, audit, update, improve, or fix CLAUDE.md files. Scans for all CLAUDE.md files, evaluates quality against templates, outputs quality report, then makes targeted updates. Also use when the user mentions "CLAUDE.md maintenance" or "project memory optimization".
- superpowers:brainstorming: You MUST use this before any creative work - creating features, building components, adding functionality, or modifying behavior. Explores user intent, requirements and design before implementation.
- superpowers:dispatching-parallel-agents: Use when facing 2+ independent tasks that can be worked on without shared state or sequential dependencies
- superpowers:executing-plans: Use when you have a written implementation plan to execute in a separate session with review checkpoints
- superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch: Use when implementation is complete, all tests pass, and you need to decide how to integrate the work - guides completion of development work by presenting structured options for merge, PR, or cleanup
- superpowers:receiving-code-review: Use when receiving code review feedback, before implementing suggestions, especially if feedback seems unclear or technically questionable - requires technical rigor and verification, not performative agreement or blind implementation
- superpowers:requesting-code-review: Use when completing tasks, implementing major features, or before merging to verify work meets requirements
- superpowers:subagent-driven-development: Use when executing implementation plans with independent tasks in the current session
- superpowers:systematic-debugging: Use when encountering any bug, test failure, or unexpected behavior, before proposing fixes
- superpowers:test-driven-development: Use when implementing any feature or bugfix, before writing implementation code
- superpowers:using-git-worktrees: Use when starting feature work that needs isolation from current workspace or before executing implementation plans - ensures an isolated workspace exists via native tools or git worktree fallback
- superpowers:using-superpowers: Use when starting any conversation - establishes how to find and use skills, requiring skill invocation before ANY response including clarifying questions
- superpowers:verification-before-completion: Use when about to claim work is complete, fixed, or passing, before committing or creating PRs - requires running verification commands and confirming output before making any success claims; evidence before assertions always
- superpowers:writing-plans: Use when you have a spec or requirements for a multi-step task, before touching code
- superpowers:writing-skills: Use when creating new skills, editing existing skills, or verifying skills work before deployment
- sonarqube:sonar-analyze: Analyze a file or code snippet for quality and security issues using SonarQube
- sonarqube:sonar-coverage: Find files with low test coverage and inspect uncovered lines in a SonarQube project (project key optional when MCP integration already defines the default project)
- sonarqube:sonar-dependency-risks: Search for software composition analysis (SCA) dependency risks in a SonarQube project (project key optional when MCP integration already defines the default project)
- sonarqube:sonar-duplication: Find files with code duplications in a SonarQube project and inspect duplication blocks for a file (project key optional when MCP integration already defines the default project)
- sonarqube:sonar-fix-issue: Fix a specific SonarQube issue in code by rule key and location
- sonarqube:sonar-integrate: Installs sonarqube-cli if not already installed, authenticates, and integrates SonarQube with the current agent (installs analysis hooks & SonarQube MCP Server). Use when the user wants to set up SonarQube integration or asks to configure SonarQube.
- sonarqube:sonar-list-issues: Search and filter SonarQube issues for a project, branch, or pull request via sonarqube-cli (`-p` is always required on the CLI; resolve the key from user arguments or sonar-project.properties)
- sonarqube:sonar-list-projects: List SonarQube projects accessible to the current user
- sonarqube:sonar-quality-gate: Show SonarQube quality gate status for a project — pass/fail and each condition (metric key, threshold, actual value). Project key optional when MCP integration already defines the default project.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- claude-in-chrome: Automates your Chrome browser to interact with web pages - clicking elements, filling forms, capturing screenshots, reading console logs, and navigating sites. Opens pages in new tabs within your existing Chrome session. Requires site-level permissions before executing (configured in the extension). - When the user wants to interact with web pages, automate browser tasks, capture screenshots, read console logs, or perform any browser-based actions. Always invoke BEFORE attempting to use any mcp__claude-in-chrome__* tools.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

clippy `-D warnings` + fmt clean.
- **Slice 4.3** ✓ shipped (PR #25, merged 2026-06-27) — Real
  user-space scheduling: kernel delegatable scheduler + SCHED server (single PR).
  `Proc` gains `scheduler: Endpoint` (`NONE` = kernel-scheduled, the boot
  default; `populate_proc` sets it and `Proc::EMPTY` zeroes it to `NONE`).
  `sched::reschedule` branches on it: `NONE` keeps the slice-2.4 refill+rotate;
  otherwise it dequeues the preempted proc (which `clock::tick`'s bare `fetch_or`
  left enqueued), leaves `RTS_NO_QUANTUM` set, and sends `SCHEDULING_NO_QUANTUM`
  to the scheduler via new `ipc::send::mini_sched_no_quantum_send` (a near-clone
  of 3.4's `mini_pf_send`, wrapped by `ipc::send_no_quantum` which materializes
  the proc-table slice like `send_pagefault_to_vm`). `SYS_SCHEDULE` becomes real
  and a new `SYS_SCHEDCTL` lands (`kernel/src/system/do_schedule.rs`), both
  target-taking and routed beside `SYS_VMCTL` in `kernel_call_dispatch`:
  `do_schedule` sets a target's priority/quantum and re-admits it
  (`rts_unset(RTS_NO_QUANTUM)` if off-queue, else dequeue+enqueue for the band
  move); `do_schedctl` claims (`scheduler = caller`) or releases
  (`SCHEDCTL_FLAG_KERNEL` → `NONE`) a target. `kernel-shared/callnr.rs` gains
  `SYS_SCHEDCTL`, `SCHEDCTL_FLAG_KERNEL`, `NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE4 = 15` (one-slice
  `_PHASE3` alias, dropped in 4.4), and a `SCHED_RQ_BASE = 0xF00` range
  (`SCHEDULING_NO_QUANTUM`/`START`/`STOP`/`SET_NICE`, `NR_SCHED_MSGS = 4`,
  const-asserted distinct from VM/SEF/DS and below `NOTIFY_MESSAGE`) + host
  tests; `init_boot_image`'s `k_call_mask` fill widens to `NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE4`
  so SCHED may issue the two calls. SCHED (`servers/sched`, server-rt based):
  SEF loop publishing to DS, handlers for all four `SCHEDULING_*`; the policy is
  a round-robin quantum refresh at a fixed managed band (`USER_Q = 8`, the
  boot-server band, so a CPU-bound managed proc round-robins instead of sinking
  behind the kernel-scheduled stubs) held in a static `[SchedProc; 16]`
  `UnsafeCell` table (`servers/sched/src/policy.rs`, pure helpers host-tested
  like `ds/registry.rs`). MINIX-style priority aging (drop a band + periodic
  `balance_queues` boost) is deferred to 4.4's `SYS_SETALARM` — without the boost,
  dropping a band would starve the managed proc behind the band-8 stubs.
  `SCHEDULING_START`/`STOP`/`SET_NICE` are implemented for PM/RS to drive from
  4.5+; in 4.3 the kernel pre-delegates stub C (`userland.rs` sets its
  `scheduler = boot_endpoint(SCHED_PROC_NR)`), the live exercise. Added to the
  MXBI `servers` array in `build.rs` (proc_nr 9). Verified in QEMU over 10 s:
  eight `[as]` lines (vm/ds/vfs/sched asid 1–4, stubs A–D asid 5–8); SCHED boots
  through SEF (`[ipc 10/11] caller=9` GET_WHOAMI + DS publish); head traces show
  the round-trip — `[noq N] proc=C nr=13 -> scheduler=0x9` (kernel delegates) and
  `[ksys SYS_SCHEDULE] target=C nr=13 prio=8 quantum=5 result=0` (SCHED
  re-admits); stub C sustains (`[ksys]` `caller=13` to sample 250 800), A↔B
  ping-pong + D's three `[pf]` (brk×2 + mmap) resolved by VM all intact; every
  line `result=0`; zero panic / `el0_sync_unexpected`. Host: `cargo test
  -p minixrs-kernel-shared -p minixrs-sched -p minixrs-server-rt` green; `cargo
  check --workspace` + clippy `-D warnings` + fmt clean.
- **Slice 4.4** ◀ ready (branch `feature/phase-4-4-rs-setalarm`, pending merge) —
  RS (reincarnation server) + real `SYS_SETALARM`. `SYS_SETALARM` replaces its
  slice-2.6 `ENOSYS` stub with a per-proc one-shot timer: `Proc` gains
  `alarm_at: u64` (absolute uptime tick, 0 = disarmed; `Proc::EMPTY` zeroes it),
  and `kernel/src/system/do_setalarm.rs` (caller-local, like the other
  non-target `SYS_*`) reads a relative `delta` (u64, payload `0..8`), stores
  `alarm_at = uptime()+delta` (disarms on `delta==0`), and replies the previous
  timer's remaining ticks. `clock.rs` gains an `EARLIEST_ALARM` fast-path gate
  (`arm_alarm`/`set_earliest_alarm`) so `tick()` stays O(1) and only pays the
  O(N) scan when an alarm is actually due; the scan + delivery live in
  `ipc::fire_expired_alarms` (a kernel-originated-delivery wrapper beside
  `send_pagefault_to_vm`/`send_no_quantum`), which clears each expired
  `alarm_at`, calls `ipc::notify::deliver_alarm`, traces `[alarm N]` (head
  carve-out + modulo, like `TRACE_HEAD`), and recomputes the next-earliest.
  `deliver_alarm` is a kernel-originated `NOTIFY` from `CLOCK` with **no `ipc_to`
  check** (CLOCK's bitmap is empty, so routing through `mini_notify` would deny
  it) — immediate when the owner is `RECEIVE`-blocked, else deferred via
  `notify_pending` against CLOCK's priv slot (drained by `mini_receive`). No new
  kernel-shared constants — the alarm reuses `NOTIFY_MESSAGE` + `CLOCK`, and
  `NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE4` stays 15. RS (`servers/rs`, server-rt based, already
  `ROOT_SYS_PROC` + `sig_mgr` with full priv wiring from `init_boot_image`) boots
  through SEF, publishes to DS, arms a periodic alarm (`ALARM_PERIOD = 100`
  ticks), and on each fire pings a static peer set (DS/VM/SCHED/VFS via
  `boot_endpoint`) with `ipc_notify`, tallying acks in a host-tested
  `servers/rs/src/monitor.rs` (`UnsafeCell` newtype like `sched/policy.rs`);
  restart-on-crash is detect-only (the `monitor::sweep` dead count — EL0 can't
  log and exec is a later slice). The RS heartbeat reuses the existing SEF ping
  (peers ack via `server-rt`'s `sef.receive`); the alarm `NOTIFY` from CLOCK is
  classified `Application` (source ≠ RS), so RS's loop keys on
  `m_source == boot_endpoint(CLOCK)`. Added to the MXBI `servers` array in
  `build.rs` (proc_nr 2); no new boot priv wiring. Verified in QEMU over 25 s:
  nine `[as]` lines (vm/ds/vfs/sched/rs asid 1–5, stubs A–D asid 6–9); head
  `[ipc]` shows RS GET_WHOAMI + DS publish (`[ipc 3/4]`), DS reply to RS
  (`[ipc 10]`), and RS's first heartbeat pings to DS/VM (`[ipc 11/12]`); six
  periodic `[alarm N] owner=r nr=2 at=100..600` fires; stub D's three `[pf]` →
  VM, SCHED `[noq]` delegation, and A↔B ping-pong + C `SYS_GETINFO` all intact;
  every line `result=0`; zero panic / `el0_sync_unexpected`. Host: `cargo test
  -p minixrs-rs -p minixrs-kernel-shared -p minixrs-server-rt` green; `cargo
  check --workspace` + clippy `-D warnings` + fmt clean.
- **Slice 4.5** ◀ next — PM part A: process table + getpid + `SYS_PRIVCTL` + minimal
  signals. Stand up PM with a static `mproc` table, `getpid`/`getppid`, and a
  kill path whose default action is terminate (no handlers/sigaction/masks —
  that's beyond Phase 4). Make `SYS_PRIVCTL` real (set up a target's priv slot;
  the 4.6 fork path leans on it). This gives the Phase-3 VM "SIGSEGV leaves
  faulter blocked" path a real consequence (PM terminates the faulter).
- **Slice 4.6** — PM part B: fork + exit + wait. Kernel `SYS_FORK` (free slot,
  copy register frame, bump generation, alloc ASID, eager AS copy by walking the
  parent's TTBR0 + copying each user page via HHDM; CoW deferred) and `SYS_EXIT`
  (tear down AS via `AddrSpace::destroy`, free slot). Forked user procs share one
  USER priv slot (MINIX's `static_priv` model) so fork can't exhaust the 64-slot
  `PRIV_TABLE`. `VM_FORK` clones the parent's `ClientRegions` into the child. PM
  owns the tree: fork → `SYS_FORK` + `VM_FORK` + `SCHEDULING_START`; exit →
  zombie + SIGCHLD; `wait`/`waitpid` → reap.
- **Slice 4.7** — exec: `SYS_EXEC` + PM exec of a boot-embedded binary. Kernel
  builds a fresh `AddrSpace`, `elf::load_into` the archive module resolved by
  `module_by_name`, sets a minimal initial user stack, swaps `ttbr0_pa` +
  destroys the old AS, sets entry/SP. PM's `execve(name)` selects the embedded
  binary. A tiny freestanding "worker" ELF packed into the archive is the exec
  target.
- **Slice 4.8** — init (PID 1) + Phase 4 wrap-up + docs. **Phase 4 complete.**
  init (a freestanding Rust ELF loaded into `INIT_PROC_NR=10` by the archive)
  forks children that exec the worker, `wait`s, and respawns in a loop. Retire or
  demote the A/B/C/D stubs now that init + real procs are the live exercise. Real
  mdBook *Servers* chapter (`book/src/servers/overview.md`); CLAUDE.md Phase-4
  conventions; flip every slice marker and write the Phase-4-complete aggregate
  paragraph.

Aggregate scope (Phase 4 as a whole):

- `minix-ipc`: NOTIFY + SENDNB primitives
- `server-rt`: SEF startup + receive loop + init/signal callbacks (minimal subset)
- Multi-module boot-image archive (MXBI) + generalized server loader
- Kernel calls made real: `SYS_FORK`, `SYS_EXEC`, `SYS_EXIT`, `SYS_PRIVCTL`,
  `SYS_SCHEDULE`, `SYS_SETALARM`, plus new `SYS_SCHEDCTL` and a minimal signal
  mechanism; delegatable scheduler (`Proc::scheduler`)
- Servers (all static-allocation, no heap): DS (endpoint registry), SCHED (real
  user-space policy), RS (heartbeat monitor), VFS (skeletal boot), PM (process
  table, fork, exec, exit, wait, getpid, minimal signals)
- init (PID 1) forking/exec/waiting; embedded "worker" binary as the exec target
- **Milestone:** Full server boot sequence completes; init process starts

### Phase 5: musl Fork + File Systems

- Add `src/minix/` to musl fork with IPC wrappers
- Generate C headers from `kernel-shared` via cbindgen
- `fs/mfs/`: MINIX File System server (Rust, MinixFS v3 on-disk format)
- `fs/pfs/`: Pipe File System
- `drivers/memory/`: /dev/null, /dev/zero, ramdisk
- initramfs for early boot before disk driver
- **Milestone:** C "Hello World" compiled against musl runs on minix.rs

### Phase 6: VirtIO Drivers

- `drivers/driver-rt/`: VirtIO MMIO transport (aarch64), virtqueue management, BDEV/CDEV protocol
- `drivers/virtio-blk/`: Block device
- `drivers/virtio-console/`: TTY
- `drivers/virtio-net/`: Network (packet I/O only; TCP/IP stack is later)
- Root filesystem on VirtIO disk
- **Milestone:** Boots from VirtIO disk, mounts MinixFS root

### Phase 7: Userland

- `userland/init/`: Run /etc/rc, respawn gettys
- `userland/sh/`: Simple shell (pipes, redirects, background jobs, builtins)
- `userland/coreutils/`: Multi-call binary -- ls, cat, cp, mv, rm, mkdir, rmdir, echo, wc, head, tail, grep, chmod, pwd, env, sleep, date, uname, kill, ps, true, false, test
- **Milestone:** Login, navigate filesystem, run scripts

---
name: Local reference repositories
description: Local paths to MINIX 3 source, musl fork, and other reference repos used for minix.rs development
type: reference
originSessionId: 465ace1e-ef33-41bc-8eec-c02bc5f92f6e
---

- **MINIX 3 (modern, with NetBSD userland):** `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/learn/minix/` -- full git history, main architectural reference. MINIX-specific code under `minix/` subdirectory (e.g., `minix/kernel/proc.c`). Has `BOOT.md` at root with Limine integration design.

input.description: Explore kernel priv/privctl/signal mechanics
input.prompt:
Explore the minix.rs kernel at /Users/kevinbarnard/src/minixrs to prepare for implementing slice 4.5: "PM part A: process table + getpid + SYS_PRIVCTL + minimal signals. Stand up PM with a static mproc table, getpid/getppid, and a kill path whose default action is terminate. Make SYS_PRIVCTL real (set up a target's priv slot; the 4.6 fork path leans on it). This gives the Phase-3 VM 'SIGSEGV leaves faulter blocked' path a real consequence (PM terminates the faulter)."

Be very thorough. Report with file:line citations:

1. `crates/kernel-shared` (or wherever it lives — find it): full contents of com.rs (proc numbers, endpoints — especially PM_PROC_NR, boot_endpoint), callnr.rs (kernel call numbers — SYS_PRIVCTL number, NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE4, request-range bases VM 0xC00 / SEF 0xD00 / DS 0xE00 / SCHED 0xF00, NOTIFY_MESSAGE), message struct layout (payload accessors, m_type/m_source).
2. Kernel proc/priv structures: `Proc` fields (esp. scheduler, alarm_at, rts_flags, endpoint/generation), `Priv` fields (ipc_to, k_call_mask, sig_mgr if any, notify_pending, flags like SRV_T/USR_T templates), the PROC_TABLE/PRIV_TABLE statics and their accessor patterns (proc_table_mut_slice etc.), RTS flag constants (RTS_PAGEFAULT, RTS_NO_QUANTUM, RTS_RECEIVING, RTS_NO_PRIV if it exists, etc.).
3. `init_boot_image` (and userland.rs / boot_image): how priv slots are assigned at boot, what priv templates exist, how ipc_to bitmaps get filled, how the stub procs A-D are installed (install_stub_d_priv etc.), which proc slots/priv slots are used, what n_active covers.
4. Kernel call dispatch: `system/mod.rs` kernel_call_dispatch — the target-taking route (SYS_VMCTL/SYS_SCHEDULE/SYS_SCHEDCTL) vs dispatch_caller_local; the current SYS_PRIVCTL stub (does it exist? what does it return?); do_schedule.rs and do_setalarm.rs as pattern references (how they parse payloads, reply, trace).
5. Termination mechanics that exist today: is there ANY path that stops/kills a proc (rts_set with what flags, dequeue, clearing a proc slot)? What does Proc::EMPTY / populate_proc look like? What would "terminate" minimally require (AS teardown exists? AddrSpace::destroy — does it exist yet, or is it slice 4.6)?
6. Page-fault path: do_page_fault → send_pagefault_to_vm / mini_pf_send — exact flow, what message VM receives, what VM can know about the faulter (endpoint? proc nr?), and how the kernel-originated send bypasses ipc_to. Also ipc::notify::deliver_alarm and send_no_quantum as kernel-originated delivery patterns.
7. Tracing conventions: [ipc N], [ksys], [alarm], [noq], [pf] — where each is emitted, head carve-out/modulo sampling patterns.

Return a structured report; include short code excerpts for the key structs/functions.

input.description: Explore servers, server-rt, build wiring
input.prompt:
Explore the minix.rs repo at /Users/kevinbarnard/src/minixrs to prepare for implementing slice 4.5, which adds a PM (process manager) server: static mproc table, getpid/getppid, a kill path with default-terminate, and wiring so VM can report a SIGSEGV faulter to PM which then terminates it.

Be very thorough. Report with file:line citations:

1. Existing servers as patterns: `servers/rs`, `servers/sched`, `servers/ds`, `servers/vfs`, `servers/vm` — for each: Cargo.toml (name, deps), main.rs structure (SEF startup config, receive loop, message dispatch, request-range constants), the pure host-tested module (rs/src/monitor.rs, sched/src/policy.rs, ds/src/registry.rs — the UnsafeCell newtype static-table pattern and its unit tests), user.ld usage. Does a `servers/pm` already exist in any form? Is PM_PROC_NR already defined and is a PM stub already in the boot image?
2. `server-rt`: sef.rs (sef_startup, SefConfig fields — init_fresh, signal_handler; Sef::receive; how SEF_SIGNAL is classified/handled), classify.rs (classification rules, what m_source gating exists, what a signal message looks like — SEF_SIGNAL constant value), ds.rs (sef_publish_to_ds), lib.rs exports. How does a server currently receive/handle a "signal"? What is the signal_handler callback signature and is anything exercising it today?
3. VM server fault handling: servers/vm main.rs + region.rs — the exact code path where an out-of-region page fault is dropped as "silent SIGSEGV" (faulter left blocked). What info does VM hold at that point (proc nr, endpoint, fault address)? What IPC edges does VM currently have (ipc_to bits — from init_boot_image or install_stub_* in the kernel)? Could VM send/notify PM today, or does that need new wiring?
4. kernel/build.rs: the `servers` array (crate, dir, proc_nr rows), build_server, pack_mxbi, rerun-if-changed lists, and what adding one more boot server requires. Also workspace Cargo.toml members list.
5. userland.rs stubs A-D: what each stub program does today (which one exercises brk/mmap/munmap — the SIGSEGV candidate; which does SYS_GETINFO; the ping-pong pair), how stub C got pre-delegated to SCHED in 4.3, and whether any stub deliberately triggers an out-of-region fault today (is there a dormant SIGSEGV demo?).
6. Test/coverage conventions: sonar-project.properties coverage exclusions (server main.rs glob, server-rt IPC modules), which crates run in CI host tests (ci.yml test job), cargo aliases in .cargo/config.toml (kernel-aarch64 etc.).

Return a structured report with short code excerpts for the key patterns (SEF loop skeleton, DS publish callback, UnsafeCell table newtype, build.rs servers array).

input.description: Explore MINIX 3 PM/privctl reference
input.prompt:
Explore the MINIX 3 reference source trees to extract the semantics minix.rs slice 4.5 should model. Two local checkouts:
- Classic (book-era, simpler): /Users/kevinbarnard/src/learn/rminix/ (kernel/, servers/, include/ at top level)
- Modern: /Users/kevinbarnard/src/learn/minix/ (code under minix/ subdirectory)

Prefer the classic tree for conceptual simplicity, cite the modern tree where it differs materially. Report with file:line citations and short excerpts:

1. PM's mproc table: servers/pm/mproc.h — fields (mp_pid, mp_parent, mp_flags, mp_endpoint if present in classic), the IN_USE flag, how slots map to kernel proc numbers. How PM initializes its table at boot (servers/pm/main.c pm_init — how it learns about boot processes).
2. getpid/getppid: servers/pm/getset.c do_get — how PID vs PPID is returned, what the reply message carries. What call numbers GETPID/GETPPID are (include/minix/callnr.h) and how the libc wrapper invokes PM (message to PM endpoint, SENDREC).
3. kill path, minimal: servers/pm/signal.c — do_kill → check_sig → sig_proc. What sig_proc does for the DEFAULT TERMINATE case with no handler installed: which kernel calls it makes (sys_kill? cause_sig in kernel? SYS_CLEAR?), and the exit path pm_exit/exit_proc — which kernel call actually destroys the process (sys_clear / SYS_CLEAR — kernel/system/do_clear.c what it does: RTS flags, slot free, IPC unblocking of procs sending to the dead one). Also how the kernel notifies PM of kernel-detected signals (cause_sig → notify PM via SIGKSIG? SYS_GETKSIG/SYS_ENDKSIG loop) — summarize briefly; we only need the minimal terminate flow.
4. How a SIGSEGV from a page fault reaches PM in real MINIX: kernel pagefault → VM → ? (does VM call sys_kill(SIGSEGV) to kernel, or notify PM?). Trace the actual chain briefly.
5. SYS_PRIVCTL: kernel/system/do_privctl.c — the request subcodes (SYS_PRIV_ALLOW, SYS_PRIV_DISALLOW, SYS_PRIV_SET_SYS, SYS_PRIV_SET_USER, SYS_PRIV_ADD_IPC, SYS_PRIV_ADD_MEM, SYS_PRIV_ID etc.), what SET_USER vs SET_SYS do (priv structure assignment — shared user priv vs per-server slot), who calls privctl (RS during service start; anyone else?), and what the USER privilege template contains (kernel/table.c / priv.h — ipc allowed to whom, no kernel calls?).
6. SYS_KILL kernel call (kernel/system/do_kill.c) + cause_sig (kernel/system.c): what they do — worth knowing whether PM→kernel kill goes through SYS_KILL or PM just uses sys_clear for terminate-default.

Keep it focused: the goal is a MINIMAL faithful subset — PM table + getpid/getppid + kill-with-default-terminate + privctl-set-user. Return a structured report.

---

# MINIX PM/kernel semantics for the minimal minixrs slice 4.5

Trees: **classic** = `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/learn/rminix/` (book-era, primary), **modern** = `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/learn/minix/minix/` (cited where it differs). Note: the classic tree here is lightly modernized (it has an async PM↔VFS protocol and endpoints), but the conceptual model is the book model.

## 1. PM's mproc table + boot-time init

**Fields** — `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/learn/rminix/servers/pm/mproc.h:9-65`. The minimal-relevant fields:
- `pid_t mp_pid` (:13) — process id
- `int mp_parent` (:17) — **index of parent process** (a slot number, not a pid)
- `endpoint_t mp_endpoint` (:16) — `endpoint: generation + proc_nr` (present in this classic tree)
- `unsigned mp_flags` (:48) — flag bits

**IN_USE flag & flags** — `mproc.h:67-84`. `#define IN_USE 0x001 /* set when 'mproc' slot in use */`. Also relevant: `ZOMBIE 0x004`, `PRIV_PROC 0x2000` (system process), `DONT_SWAP 0x1000`.

**Slot↔kernel proc number mapping**: mproc is indexed by the same process number as the kernel proc table and FS table — stated in the header comment (`mproc.h:1-6`: "The kernel and file systems have tables that are also indexed by process, with the contents of corresponding slots referring to the same process in all three"). Endpoint is built as `_ENDPOINT(0, proc_nr)`.

**Boot init** — `pm_init()` at `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/learn/rminix/servers/pm/main.c:353-469`:
- Clears the whole table / timers (`:381-385`).
- Learns about boot processes by asking the **kernel for a copy of the boot image table**: `sys_getimage(image)` (`:416`), iterating `image[0..NR_BOOT_PROCS]` (`:420`).
- For each image entry with `proc_nr >= 0` (skips negative-numbered tasks) it fills `rmp = &mproc[ip->proc_nr]` (`:425`), sets `mp_endpoint = _ENDPOINT(0, ip->proc_nr)` (`:427`), `mp_parent = RS_PROC_NR` (`:428`).
- INIT gets `mp_pid = INIT_PID` and `mp_flags |= IN_USE` (`:430-433`); every other (system) process gets `mp_pid = get_free_pid()` and `mp_flags |= IN_USE | DONT_SWAP | PRIV_PROC` (`:436-437`).
- PM patches itself afterward: `mproc[PM_PROC_NR].mp_pid = PM_PID; mproc[PM_PROC_NR].mp_parent = PM_PROC_NR` (`:463-464`) — PM has no parent.

`PM_PROC_NR = 0` and `PM_PROC_EP = _ENDPOINT(0, PM_PROC_NR)` — `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/learn/rminix/include/minix/com.h:34,51`.

*Modern diff:* same shape; `mproc.h` there also carries scheduling/endpoint fields, and init is split into an `sef_startup`/`do_init` path, but the `sys_getimage` boot-image loop is conceptually identical.

## 2. getpid / getppid

**One call returns both.** `do_getset()` — `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/learn/rminix/servers/pm/getset.c:38-41`:
```c
case GETPID:
    r = mproc[who].mp_pid;                              /* primary result = PID */
    rmp->mp_reply.reply_res2 = mproc[rmp->mp_parent].mp_pid;  /* secondary = PPID */
```

## Minimal faithful subset — recommended model for minixrs 4.5

- **mproc table** indexed by proc-number == kernel proc slot: `{ mp_pid, mp_parent (slot index), mp_flags with IN_USE, mp_endpoint }`. Seed it from a boot image (INIT + servers) exactly as `pm_init` does (`main.c:420-464`), with PM self-patched to `pid=PM_PID, parent=self`.
- **getpid/getppid**: single PM call `GETPID`; return `mp_pid` as the result and `mproc[mp_parent].mp_pid` as a secondary reply field.
- **kill (default terminate)**: `do_kill → check_sig → sig_proc`; for the no-handler default, `pm_exit → sys_exit/SYS_CLEAR`, whose kernel side sets the slot `SLOT_FREE`, bumps the endpoint generation, and unblocks any process blocked sending-to / receiving-from the dead endpoint (`do_exit.c:63-102` / `do_clear.c:49-57`).
- **privctl SET_USER**: point the target proc at the single shared `USER_PRIV_ID` priv structure — trap mask + ipc-to restricted to servers, and an **empty kernel `call_mask`** (no kernel calls for user processes).

- `int mp_parent` (:17) — **index of parent process** (a slot number, not a pid)
- `endpoint_t mp_endpoint` (:16) — `endpoint: generation + proc_nr` (present in this classic tree)
- `unsigned mp_flags` (:48) — flag bits

**IN_USE flag & flags** — `mproc.h:67-84`. `#define IN_USE 0x001 /* set when 'mproc' slot in use */`. Also relevant: `ZOMBIE 0x004`, `PRIV_PROC 0x2000` (system process), `DONT_SWAP 0x1000`.

**Slot↔kernel proc number mapping**: mproc is indexed by the same process number as the kernel proc table and FS table — stated in the header comment (`mproc.h:1-6`: "The kernel and file systems have tables that are also indexed by process, with the contents of corresponding slots referring to the same process in all three"). So: **page fault → kernel → VM (`do_pagefaults`/`handle_pagefault`) → `sys_kill(ep, SIGSEGV)` → kernel `cause_sig` → PM.** VM does *not* call PM directly; it goes back through the kernel.

## 5. SYS_PRIVCTL (SET_USER vs SET_SYS)

**Classic** `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/learn/rminix/kernel/system/do_privctl.c`. Subcodes (`/Users/kevinbarnard/src/learn/rminix/include/minix/com.h:438-443`): `SYS_PRIV_INIT=1`, `SYS_PRIV_ADD_IO=2`, `SYS_PRIV_ADD_MEM=3`, `SYS_PRIV_ADD_IRQ=4`, `SYS_PRIV_USER=5`. Handler switch at `do_privctl.c:50`.

- Caller must itself be a system process: `if (!(priv(caller_ptr)->s_flags & SYS_PROC)) return EPERM` (`:45`). Only **RS** (which has full call rights) drives service startup; other system procs can only privctl a process frozen by `NO_PRIV` (`:39-43`, `:53`).
- **SYS_PRIV_INIT (≈ modern SET_SYS)** — `:52-132`: gives the target its **own** priv slot via `get_priv(rp, SYS_PROC)` (`:59`), copies the caller's priv template (`*priv(rp) = *priv(caller_ptr)`, `:61`), restores its own id/proc_nr, clears pending state, sets `s_trap_mask = FILLED_MASK` and full `s_ipc_to` except the user id (`unset_sys_bit(...s_ipc_to, USER_PRIV_ID)`, `:71-75`), then optionally copies IRQ/IO ranges and `s_call_mask` from a user-supplied `struct priv` (`:93-128`). Finally clears `NO_PRIV` so it can run (`:131`).
- **SYS_PRIV_USER (≈ modern SET_USER)** — `:134-140`: `get_priv(rp, 0)` then clear `NO_PRIV`. That makes it an ordinary user process sharing the **single global user priv structure**.

**get_priv** — `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/learn/rminix/kernel/system.c:196-218`: `SYS_PROC` → scan for a free slot (`s_proc_nr==NONE && s_id!=USER_PRIV_ID`), assign it, set `s_flags = SYS_PROC` (`:205-211`). Otherwise → `rc->p_priv = &priv[USER_PRIV_ID]` (the **shared** slot), `s_flags = 0` (`:212-216`). `USER_PRIV_ID = 0` — `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/learn/rminix/kernel/priv.h:92`.

**struct priv** — `priv.h:28-57`: `s_flags`, `s_trap_mask` (allowed IPC primitives), `s_ipc_from`/`s_ipc_to` (allowed IPC peers, bitmaps over priv ids), `s_call_mask[]` (allowed kernel calls), plus IO/mem/IRQ tables. Flag bits `priv.h:62-68` (`SYS_PROC=0x10`, etc.).

**USER privilege template** — the user process is initialized from the boot image row `INIT` in `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/learn/rminix/kernel/table.c:104`:
```
{ INIT_PROC_NR, 0, USR_F, 8, USER_Q, 0, USR_T, USR_M, {0}, "init" },
```
Note the **`call_mask` is `{0}`** — user processes have **no kernel calls allowed**. Its trap mask is `USR_T` and its ipc-to mask `USR_M` (very restricted — user procs may only IPC the servers, not arbitrary system procs). `init_boot_call_masks()` (`table.c:121-...`) leaves IDLE/CLOCK/SYSTEM/HARDWARE/**INIT** call masks all-zero (`:128-130`), fills RS's mask completely (`:133`, RS distributes rights), gives PM everything except device I/O (`:136-143`), and grants VFS/drivers a small explicit set (`:146-166`, e.g. `SYS_KILL`, `SYS_EXIT`, copies).

*Modern diff* — `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/learn/minix/minix/include/minix/com.h:342-352` renames/expands: `SYS_PRIV_ALLOW=1`, `SYS_PRIV_DISALLOW=2`, `SYS_PRIV_SET_SYS=3`, `SYS_PRIV_SET_USER=4`, `ADD_IO=5`, `ADD_MEM=6`, `ADD_IRQ=7`, `QUERY_MEM=8`, `YIELD=10` (no `SYS_PRIV_ADD_IPC` in this version). `SYS_PRIV_SET_USER` is a one-liner: `priv(rp) = priv_addr(USER_PRIV_ID)` (`/Users/kevinbarnard/src/learn/minix/minix/kernel/system/do_privctl.c:176-185`) — same "share the global user priv" semantics as classic `get_priv(rp,0)`. `SET_SYS` there allocates static-or-dynamic priv ids and calls `update_priv()`/`fill_sendto_mask()` (`:86-150, 280-353`).

## 6. SYS_KILL kernel call + cause_sig

**do_kill (kernel)** — `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/learn/rminix/kernel/system/do_kill.c:18-44`. Params `SIG_PROC` (target proc nr) and `SIG_NUMBER`. Validation `:32-33` (reject bad proc / kernel targets). Two behaviors (`:35-42`):
- If the caller is **PM itself** (`m_source == PM_PROC_EP`): deliver directly to a system process via `send_sig(proc_nr, sig_nr)` (the sig2mess/notify route, `:38`).
- Otherwise (any *other* system process, e.g. VFS/VM/TTY): `cause_sig(proc_nr, sig_nr)` (`:41`) — queue it for PM.

**cause_sig** — `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/learn/rminix/kernel/system.c:273-301`: adds to `p_pending`, and if not already SIGNALED sets `SIGNALED | SIG_PENDING` and `send_sig(PM_PROC_NR, SIGKSIG)` (`:294-299`) so PM's ksig loop picks it up.

**Answer to the design question**: for a normal **kill-with-default-terminate**, PM does **not** route through `SYS_KILL`. `SYS_KILL` is the *inbound* path (userland `kill(2)` → PM `do_kill`; and other servers/kernel → PM via `cause_sig`). Once PM decides the disposition is "terminate", it calls `pm_exit → sys_exit` (modern `sys_clear`), i.e. **`SYS_EXIT`/`SYS_CLEAR` is what actually tears the process down**. PM only uses `sys_kill` (SYS_KILL) for the *sig2mess* case (delivering a signal-as-notification to a system process), `signal.c:479`.

---

## Minimal faithful subset — recommended model for minixrs 4.5

- **mproc table** indexed by proc-number == kernel proc slot: `{ mp_pid, mp_parent (slot index), mp_flags with IN_USE, mp_endpoint }`. Seed it from a boot image (INIT + servers) exactly as `pm_init` does (`main.c:420-464`), with PM self-patched to `pid=PM_PID, parent=self`.
- **getpid/getppid**: single PM call `GETPID`; return `mp_pid` as the result and `mproc[mp_parent].mp_pid` as a secondary reply field.
- **kill (default terminate)**: `do_kill → check_sig → sig_proc`; for the no-handler default, `pm_exit → sys_exit/SYS_CLEAR`, whose kernel side sets the slot `SLOT_FREE`, bumps the endpoint generation, and unblocks any process blocked sending-to / receiving-from the dead endpoint (`do_exit.c:63-102` / `do_clear.c:49-57`).
- **privctl SET_USER**: point the target proc at the single shared `USER_PRIV_ID` priv structure — trap mask + ipc-to restricted to servers, and an **empty kernel `call_mask`** (no kernel calls for user processes).

All citations are absolute paths with line numbers above.</result>

## Exploration notes

### MINIX 3 reference semantics (what to model, minimally)

- **mproc**: indexed by proc number == kernel proc slot; fields `{mp_pid, mp_parent (slot index), mp_endpoint, mp_flags: IN_USE}`. PM seeds it at boot from the boot image (servers get PRIV_PROC pids, PM patches itself `parent=self`).
- **getpid/getppid**: ONE call (`GETPID`); reply carries PID as result and PPID in a secondary payload field (`getset.c do_get`); libc `getppid` reads the secondary field.
- **kill → default terminate**: `do_kill → check_sig → sig_proc`; with no handler the disposition falls to terminate → exit path → kernel `SYS_CLEAR`/`SYS_EXIT` (`do_clear.c`): cancel timer, dequeue, unblock procs sending-to/receiving-from the dead endpoint, bump generation, set slot free, release priv. PM does NOT use SYS_KILL for the terminate default — SYS_KILL is the inbound path (other servers → `cause_sig` → notify PM).
- **SIGSEGV chain (modern MINIX w/ VM)**: pagefault → VM `handle_pagefault` fails → VM calls `sys_kill(ep, SIGSEGV)` → kernel `cause_sig` → PM notified (SIGKSIG) → PM `check_sig/sig_proc` → terminate. VM never talks to PM directly.
- **SYS_PRIVCTL**: `SET_SYS` = own priv slot copied from template + full setup; `SET_USER` = point target at the single shared USER priv slot (restricted ipc_to, EMPTY kernel call mask). Caller must be a system proc (RS/PM).

## Plan

(TBD after exploration)

# minix.rs — Slice 4.5 Preparation Report (PM part A)

Crates live at the **workspace root**, not under `crates/`. The shared crate is `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/minixrs/kernel-shared/` (package `minixrs-kernel-shared`, imported as `minixrs_kernel_shared`). The kernel is `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/minixrs/kernel/`.

Key status finding up front: **PM is not yet real.** `servers/pm/src/main.rs` is a 6-line `fn main(){ /* TODO */ }`, and `kernel/build.rs:105-111` packs only VM/DS/VFS/SCHED/RS into the boot archive — PM (proc 0) is absent, so its boot slot stays blocked on `RTS_NO_PRIV`. There is **no `mproc` table, no getpid, no terminate/kill path, and `SYS_PRIVCTL` is an `ENOSYS` stub** anywhere in the tree. Slice 4.5 builds all of these.

---

## 1. `kernel-shared` — proc numbers, call numbers, message layout

### com.rs (`kernel-shared/src/com.rs`)
- Counts: `NR_TASKS=5` (:14), `NR_PROCS=1024` (:16), `NR_SYS_PROCS=64` (:18).
- Kernel tasks (negative): `ASYNCM=-5, IDLE=-4, CLOCK=-3, SYSTEM=-2, HARDWARE=-1` (:30-38).
- Servers (gen-0 boot): **`PM_PROC_NR=ProcNr::new(0)`** (:48), `VFS=1, RS=2, MEM=3, TTY=4, DS=5, MFS=6, VM=7, PFS=8, SCHED=9, INIT=10` (:49-58).
- `NR_BOOT_PROCS = INIT+1 = 11` (:62).
- **`boot_endpoint(p)`** (:69-71) = `make_endpoint(0, p)` — the const used everywhere to name a boot proc/task; PM's endpoint is `boot_endpoint(PM_PROC_NR)`.

### callnr.rs (`kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs`)
- `KERNEL_CALL = 0x600` (:11). Calls are contiguous:
  - `SYS_GETINFO=0x600` (:14), **`SYS_PRIVCTL=0x601`** (:15), `SYS_FORK=0x602`, `SYS_EXEC=0x603`, `SYS_EXIT=0x604`, `SYS_COPY, SYS_SAFECOPY, SYS_IRQCTL, SYS_VMCTL=0x608, SYS_SCHEDULE=0x609, SYS_SETALARM=0x60A, SYS_TIMES, SYS_DIAGCTL, SYS_SETGRANT, SYS_SCHEDCTL=0x60E` (:14-33).
- **`NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE4 = 15`** (:42); `NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE3` is a one-slice alias equal to it (:48). `NR_SYS_CALLS = 32` (:53) — width of `k_call_mask` (one u32 word).
- `GET_WHOAMI=12` (:70), `SYS_GETINFO_NAME_LEN=16` (:76).
- Request-range bases (all *server IPC* m_type ranges, distinct from `KERNEL_CALL`, all `< NOTIFY_MESSAGE`):
  - **VM `VM_RQ_BASE=0xC00`** (:129) — `VM_PAGEFAULT=0xC00`, `VM_BRK, VM_MMAP, VM_MUNMAP` (:135-160).
  - **SEF `SEF_RQ_BASE=0xD00`** (:233) — `SEF_INIT=0xD00`, **`SEF_SIGNAL=0xD01`** (:237-242), signal number in payload `0..4`.
  - **DS `DS_RQ_BASE=0xE00`** (:184).
  - **SCHED `SCHED_RQ_BASE=0xF00`** (:270) — `SCHEDULING_NO_QUANTUM=0xF00` (kernel-originated), `SCHEDULING_START=0xF01`, `SCHEDULING_STOP`, `SCHEDULING_SET_NICE` (:275-290). `SCHEDULING_START` doc (:277-281) explicitly says "Driven by PM/RS from slice 4.5+".
  - `SCHEDCTL_FLAG_KERNEL = 1<<0` (:38).
- `NOTIFY_MESSAGE = 0x1000` lives in `ipc_const.rs:28` (with `SEND=1, RECEIVE=2, SENDREC=3, NOTIFY=4, SENDNB=5, SENDA=16`).

### message.rs (`kernel-shared/src/message.rs`)
```rust
#[repr(C, align(8))]
pub struct Message { pub m_source: Endpoint, pub m_type: i32, pub payload: [u8; 96] } // 104 bytes
```
(:33-42; `m_source`/`m_type` at offsets 0/4, `payload` at offset 8.) **No typed accessors** — every handler reads/writes payload with `i32::from_ne_bytes(msg.payload[off..off+4])` / `u64::from_ne_bytes(...)` and `copy_from_slice(&v.to_ne_bytes())` (see the `read_i32`/`write_u64` helpers repeated in `do_schedule.rs:173-180`, `do_vmctl.rs:252-278`, `do_setalarm.rs:61-73`).

### endpoint.rs (`kernel-shared/src/endpoint.rs`)
- `Endpoint = i32` (wire format, :26); `ProcNr(i32)` (:40, `.new/.get/.is_task`), `PrivId(u16)` (:65, `.new/.get/.as_usize`), `SysId` alias of `PrivId` (:88).
- `ENDPOINT_GEN_SHIFT=15` (:92); `make_endpoint(g,p)` (:115), `endpoint_proc(e)` sign-extends so tasks come back negative (:121-130), `endpoint_gen(e)` (:133-135).
- Sentinels `ANY`, `NONE`, `SELF` all at gen 0 (:105-112).

---

## 2. Kernel `Proc` / `Priv` structures and statics

### `Proc` (`kernel/src/proc/proc_struct.rs:27-120`)
Fields relevant to 4.5: `nr: ProcNr`, `endpoint: Endpoint`, `priv_id: Option<PrivId>`, `rts_flags: AtomicU32` (:37), `misc_flags: u32`, `priority: u8`, `quantum_ms/quantum_left`, `user_time/sys_time`, **`alarm_at: u64`** (:57), IPC state (`caller_q/q_link: Option<ProcNr>`, `getfrom_e/sendto_e`, `send_msg/deliver_msg/deliver_msg_vir`), `ttbr0_pa/asid`, `page_fault_state/heap_window`, **`scheduler: Endpoint`** (:110, `NONE`=kernel-scheduled), `next_ready: Option<ProcNr>`, `name: [u8;16]`.
`Proc::EMPTY` (:127-162) initializes `rts_flags = RTS_SLOT_FREE`, `endpoint = NONE`, `priv_id = None`, `scheduler = NONE`.

### `Priv` (`kernel/src/proc/priv_struct.rs:51-84`)
```rust
pub struct Priv {
    pub id: PrivId, pub proc_nr: Option<ProcNr>, pub flags: u16, pub trap_mask: u16,
    pub ipc_to: [u32; 2], pub k_call_mask: [u32; 1],
    pub notify_pending: [u32; 2], pub asyn_pending: [u32; 2],
    pub sig_mgr: Endpoint,                      // :72 — signal manager endpoint
    pub io_ranges/mem_ranges/irqs: ArrayVec<…>, pub grant_table: u64, pub grant_entries: u32,
}
```
`IPC_MAP_CHUNKS=2`, `K_CALL_MASK_CHUNKS=1` (:26-28). `Priv::EMPTY` sets `sig_mgr = NONE` (:88-103). Note `sig_mgr` already exists — at boot it is set to RS's endpoint for every slot (see §3).

### Flags (`kernel/src/proc/flags.rs`)
- RTS (block reasons; runnable iff `rts_flags==0`): `RTS_SLOT_FREE=0x1` (:33), `RTS_PROC_STOP=0x2`, `RTS_SENDING=0x4`, `RTS_RECEIVING=0x8`, `RTS_SIGNALED=0x10`, `RTS_SIG_PENDING=0x20`, `RTS_P_STOP=0x40`, **`RTS_NO_PRIV=0x80`** (:47, "forked sysproc awaiting priv assignment"), `RTS_NO_ENDPOINT=0x100` (:49, "cannot send/receive — no valid endpoint"), `RTS_VMINHIBIT=0x200`, **`RTS_PAGEFAULT=0x400`** (:53), `RTS_VMREQUEST/TARGET`, `RTS_PREEMPTED=0x4000`, **`RTS_NO_QUANTUM=0x8000`**, `RTS_BOOTINHIBIT=0x10000`.
- Priv flags (:91-111): `PREEMPTIBLE=0x2, BILLABLE=0x4, DYN_PRIV_ID=0x8` (:95, "assigned dynamically, not from boot image"), `SYS_PROC=0x10`, `CHECK_IO_PORT/IRQ/MEM`, `ROOT_SYS_PROC=0x100`, `VM_SYS_PROC=0x200`, `RST_SYS_PROC=0x800`.
- Trap masks (:118-124): `TSK_T=0`, `CSK_T=1<<RECEIVE`, `USR_T=1<<SENDREC`, **`SRV_T=!0`** (all primitives).

### Statics + accessors (`kernel/src/proc/table.rs`)
- `N_PROC_SLOTS = NR_TASKS + NR_PROCS = 1029` (:30). Priority bands: `TASK_Q=0, RS_Q=4, VM_Q=4, SRV_Q=8, INIT_Q=8, IDLE_Q=15` (:37-47), `SRV_QUANTUM_MS=200` (:50).
- `PROC_TABLE: ProcStorage(UnsafeCell<[Proc; N_PROC_SLOTS]>)` and `PRIV_TABLE: PrivStorage(UnsafeCell<[Priv; NR_SYS_PROCS]>)` (:68-71), `#[repr(transparent)]` + `unsafe impl Sync` (single-threaded boot invariant).
- `proc_index(nr)` (:78-86, adds `NR_TASKS`), `priv_index(id)` (:89-92).
- Accessors (:266-316): `proc_slot_mut(nr)`, `proc_table_ref()`, **`proc_table_mut_slice()`**, `priv_slot_mut(id)`, `priv_table_ref()`, **`priv_table_mut_slice()`** — all `unsafe`, carrying the single-threaded invariant. Convention (CLAUDE.md:126): only `ipc::do_ipc` materializes the full slices; primitives take explicit `&mut [Proc; N_PROC_SLOTS]`/`&mut [Priv; NR_SYS_PROCS]`.
- Bitmap helpers (`kernel/src/proc/bitmap.rs`): `set_sys_bit/get_sys_bit(map, PrivId)` (priv-indexed) and `set_call_bit/get_call_bit(map, usize)` (call-indexed); all no-op/false on OOB (:28-63).

---

## 3. `init_boot_image` — how priv/proc slots are assigned at boot

`table.rs::init()` (:327-330) → `init_empty_slots()` (:332-350, gives every slot a valid `(nr, endpoint)` and every priv slot its `id`) → **`init_boot_image()`** (:352-362).

- `IMAGE: [BootEntry; 16]` (:111-258): 5 kernel tasks + 11 boot procs (PM..INIT). Each `BootEntry { nr, name, priv_flags, trap_mask, priority, quantum_ms, runnable }` (:98-106). **PM's entry** (:159-167): `priv_flags = SYS_PROC|PREEMPTIBLE`, `trap_mask = SRV_T`, `priority = SRV_Q`, `runnable = false`.
- **`n_active = N_IMAGE = 16`** (:355) — the count `populate_priv` uses to fill `ipc_to`.
- `populate_priv(id, entry, n_active)` (:364-386): priv slot index == IMAGE index (PM → priv slot 5). For `SRV_T` slots it `fill_bits(ipc_to, 16)` (send to priv slots 0..15) and `fill_bits(k_call_mask, NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE4=15)` (**every kernel call, incl. SYS_PRIVCTL**). **`sig_mgr = boot_endpoint(RS_PROC_NR)`** for every slot (:383).
- `populate_proc` (:388-412): sets `priv_id`, priority, quantum, endpoint, `scheduler = NONE`, name, and `rts_flags = RTS_NO_PRIV` (boot servers start blocked until an ELF loads).

**Consequence for PM:** its proc slot (0) and priv slot (5, SRV_T, full ipc_to over slots 0-15, full k_call_mask) are already wired by `init_boot_image`. But `ipc_to` covers only priv slots 0..15 — **not** the stub priv slots 16-19. To *signal* a stub (SEF_SIGNAL send) PM would need that reverse edge opened, exactly as `install_stub_d_priv` opens VM→D (see below). A kernel *terminate* of a stub via `SYS_PRIVCTL`/new call needs no ipc_to edge.

### Stub install (`kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs`)
- Stub proc nrs: `STUB_A=11, B=12, C=13, D=14` (:76-82); priv ids `A=16, B=17, C=18, D=19` (:86-89).
- `userland_bootstrap()` (:132-242): loads MXBI servers via `load_boot_server` (:265-314, clears `RTS_NO_PRIV`, enqueues), `build_stub` ×4, pre-delegates stub C to SCHED (`p.scheduler = boot_endpoint(SCHED_PROC_NR)`, :218-221), then `install_stub_privs()` (:463-480).
- `install_stub_c_priv` (:488-512): `USR_T`, `ipc_to`→SYSTEM only, `k_call_mask`→`SYS_GETINFO` only. `sig_mgr = RS`.
- **`install_stub_d_priv` (:530-563)**: `USR_T`, `ipc_to`→VM only, empty `k_call_mask`; then **opens the reverse VM→D edge** with a separate `priv_slot_mut(vm_priv_id)` borrow and `set_sys_bit(&mut vm_pr.ipc_to, STUB_D_PRIV_ID)` (:559-562). This is the reference pattern for opening a cross-priv IPC edge (e.g. PM→stub for signal delivery). Stub D (priv 19) is the process that takes the VM SIGSEGV path.

---

## 4. Kernel-call dispatch (`kernel/src/system/mod.rs`)

- `kernel_call_sendrec` (:70-135): the `SENDREC → SYSTEM` fast path. Enforces the same `ipc_to` gate as `mini_send` (:94-99), copies request from user (`copy_msg_from_user`), **stamps `msg.m_source` with the verified caller endpoint** (:109), dispatches, then writes the reply (`m_source = SYSTEM`, `m_type = result`) back to the user buffer, emitting the `[ksys N]` trace (:124-132).
- `kernel_call_dispatch` (:147-182): range-checks `m_type - KERNEL_CALL` against `NR_SYS_CALLS` (→`EBADREQUEST`), gates on `k_call_mask` via `get_call_bit` (→`ECALLDENIED`), then a **target-taking match** routes `SYS_VMCTL / SYS_SCHEDULE / SYS_SCHEDCTL` (:170-175) — these get the whole `&mut [Proc; N_PROC_SLOTS]` + `caller_nr`. Everything else falls to `dispatch_caller_local`.
- `dispatch_caller_local` (:188-210): const-asserts `NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE4 == 15` to force a new arm per new call. **`SYS_PRIVCTL => stubs::do_privctl(caller, caller_priv, msg)`** (:195).

**Current `SYS_PRIVCTL` (`kernel/src/system/stubs.rs:25`):** `enosys_stub!(do_privctl)` → returns `ENOSYS` (:17-23). Signature is caller-local: `fn(_caller: &mut Proc, _caller_priv: &Priv, _msg: &mut Message) -> i32`. **To make it real and act on a *target* named in the message (MINIX `SYS_PRIVCTL` targets another proc), it must move to the target-taking route** — add a `SYS_PRIVCTL => do_privctl::do_privctl(proc_table, caller_nr, msg)` arm in `kernel_call_dispatch` alongside VMCTL/SCHEDULE (:170-175), taking `&mut [Proc; N_PROC_SLOTS]` (and likely a `&mut [Priv]` — note the current signature passes only `&Priv`; the dispatch call site at :179-181 borrows `priv_table` immutably, so wiring PRIVCTL to mutate priv slots needs the mutable priv slice like `kernel_call_sendrec` holds at :71).

### Pattern references

**`do_schedule.rs`** (target-taking): `do_schedule` (:71-131) and `do_schedctl` (:140-171). Reads target endpoint (`SELF`→caller) at payload `0..4` via `read_i32`, resolves `endpoint_proc → proc_index` (→`EINVAL` if OOB), mutates the slot, then uses the **capture-then-borrow-end** `sched::rts_unset`/`dequeue`/`enqueue` pattern (:104-119). Head-carve trace `[ksys SYS_SCHEDULE]` with `SCHED_TRACE_HEAD=6` (:52-58, :121-129). `do_schedctl` snapshots the caller endpoint before mutably borrowing the target (:159-162) to handle self-targeting.

**`do_setalarm.rs`** (caller-local): `do_setalarm(caller, _priv, msg)` (:38-59) reads `delta` (u64) at payload `0..8`, computes `at = now + delta`, sets `caller.alarm_at`, calls `clock::arm_alarm(at)`, replies with previous remaining ticks at payload `0..8`. `read_u64`/`write_u64` helpers (:61-73). This is the template for a caller-local getpid-style handler if any PM logic were kernel-side (it won't be — getpid lives in PM's mproc table).

**`do_vmctl.rs`** documents the trust model (:17-28): the only gate is `k_call_mask`; a caller reaching the handler may name any target. This is the model `SYS_PRIVCTL` will inherit (PM is the sole intended holder).

---

## 5. Termination mechanics that exist today

**There is no kill/terminate/exit path.** Nothing clears a proc slot, frees a priv slot, or bumps a generation. The building blocks that exist:

- **`sched::rts_set(p, flag)`** (`kernel/src/proc/sched.rs:200-208`): `fetch_or`s the flag; if the proc was runnable it `dequeue`s it. Converse `rts_unset` (:220-228) enqueues when all bits clear. These are the capture-`nr`-then-re-borrow helpers (CLAUDE.md:128).
- `dequeue(nr)` (:137-184) splices a proc out of its run-queue band; `pick_proc` (:235-239). `schedule_next` (:292-320) installs the next proc's TTBR0/ASID and asserts `ttbr0_pa != 0 && asid != 0`.
- **A minimal "terminate"** (default action of the 4.5 kill path) needs, at minimum: `rts_set` a permanent block flag (candidates: `RTS_NO_ENDPOINT=0x100`, `RTS_PROC_STOP=0x2`, or a new terminate flag) so `dequeue` pulls it off the run queue and it never runs again. That already gives "the faulter stops." Fuller teardown (reclaim slot, generation bump for reuse) is slice 4.6 territory.
- `Proc::EMPTY` (`proc_struct.rs:127-162`, `rts_flags=RTS_SLOT_FREE`) and `populate_proc`/`populate_stub_slot` show what a slot holds; there is **no `clear_slot`/free helper yet**.
- **`AddrSpace::destroy` DOES exist** (`kernel/src/arch/aarch64/addrspace.rs:149-155`: `free_subtree(ttbr0_pa,0)` + `free_frame(L0)`), but it is **never called at runtime** — every boot server and stub `mem::forget`s its `AddrSpace` (userland.rs:310, :384) precisely so `destroy` won't run. The plan reserves `AddrSpace::destroy` for `SYS_EXIT` teardown in **slice 4.6** (docs/plan.md:963-965). So a 4.5 terminate can leave the AS mapped (just block the proc); AS teardown is explicitly a later slice.

**The consequence 4.5 gives real:** VM's SIGSEGV path (`servers/vm/src/main.rs:130-135`): an out-of-region fault returns without `VMCTL_PT_MAP`/`VMCTL_CLEAR_PAGEFAULT`, leaving the faulter blocked on `RTS_PAGEFAULT` forever. Region check is `region::contains(nr, far)` (:132). Comment at :126-129 literally names the gap: "the only 'kill' available until PM + signals in Phase 4." PM terminating the faulter is the real consequence.

---

## 6. Page-fault path and kernel-originated delivery

- **`do_page_fault`** (`kernel/src/arch/aarch64/exception.rs:148-216`): decodes EC (0x20 instr / 0x24 data abort — else halt via `el0_sync_unexpected`), builds `flags` (`PFF_INSTR/PFF_WRITE/PFF_PERMISSION`), **permission faults halt** (:176-178). Under a tightly-scoped borrow it records `p.page_fault_state = {addr:far, flags, ip:elr}` and `sched::rts_set(p, RTS_PAGEFAULT)` (:186-200), captures `faulting_nr`/`name`, then calls `crate::ipc::send_pagefault_to_vm(faulting_nr, far, flags)` (:208) and prints **`[pf] proc=… far=… flags=… -> VM`** every fault (:210-215).
- `send_pagefault_to_vm` (`kernel/src/ipc/mod.rs:289-295`) materializes the proc table and calls **`send::mini_pf_send`** (`kernel/src/ipc/send.rs:139-190`): builds `Message { m_source = faulter endpoint, m_type = VM_PAGEFAULT, payload: far@0..8 (u64), flags@8..12 (u32) }`. **No `ipc_to`/permission check and no deadlock check** — kernel-originated (:134-138). If VM `will_receive`, deliver immediately (`deliver_msg`, `MF_DELIVERMSG`, `rts_unset RTS_RECEIVING`); else queue the faulter on VM's `caller_q` with `rts_set RTS_SENDING` (RTS_PAGEFAULT already keeps it blocked).
- **What VM knows about the faulter:** only `m_source` (the faulter's endpoint). VM does `endpoint_proc(faulting_e).get()` → proc nr (`vm/main.rs:130-131`) and gates on its region table. It can also pull fault addr/flags/ip via `SYS_VMCTL(VMCTL_GET_PAGEFAULT)` (`do_vmctl.rs:218-232`).
- **Sibling kernel-originated patterns** (all bypass `ipc_to`, same shape PM's signal delivery to a `sig_mgr`/target could mimic):
  - `notify::deliver_alarm` (`kernel/src/ipc/notify.rs:119-155`): kernel `NOTIFY` from `CLOCK`, no permission check; immediate-deliver-or-set-`notify_pending`.
  - `send_no_quantum` (`ipc/mod.rs:305-311`) → `mini_sched_no_quantum_send` (`send.rs:212-264`): kernel SEND of `SCHEDULING_NO_QUANTUM` from the preempted proc to its `scheduler`, `m_source` = preempted proc.
  - `will_receive(dst, src_e)` (`notify.rs:29-36`) and `build_notify_message` (:41-47) are the shared delivery predicates.

---

## 7. Tracing conventions

All traces use an `AtomicU64` counter + a **head carve-out** (first N unconditionally) and/or **modulo sampling**; head carve-out exists because stub C's `SYS_GETINFO` flood would otherwise starve the modulo sampler of rare events.

- **`[ipc N]`** — `ipc/mod.rs::do_ipc` (:113-120). `TRACE_HEAD=12` (:70) + `TRACE_EVERY=100` (:62): `if n <= TRACE_HEAD || n % TRACE_EVERY == 0`.
- **`[ksys N]`** — `system/mod.rs::kernel_call_sendrec` (:124-132). `KCALL_TRACE_EVERY=100` (:57), modulo only. Plus per-handler head-carve variants: `[ksys SYS_SCHEDULE]` (`do_schedule.rs:121-129`, `SCHED_TRACE_HEAD=6`), `[ksys VMCTL_PT_MAP]`/`[ksys VMCTL_PT_UNMAP]` (`do_vmctl.rs:157-201`, `VMCTL_TRACE_HEAD=6`).
- **`[alarm N]`** — `ipc/mod.rs::trace_alarm_fire` (:375-387). `ALARM_TRACE_HEAD=6` + `ALARM_TRACE_EVERY=100` (:315-320); prints only on head or modulo (`owner=… nr=… at=…`).
- **`[noq N]`** — `proc/sched.rs::reschedule` (:392-403). `NOQ_TRACE_HEAD=6` (:39), head-only (delegated quantum-exhaust events are rare).
- **`[pf]`** — `exception.rs::do_page_fault` (:210-215). **Unsampled** — printed on every fault.
- **`[as]`** — boot address-space summary: `userland.rs::load_boot_server` (:298-306) and `print_addrspace_summary` (:604-611).

New 4.5 traces (PM lives at EL0 and cannot print) should follow the same kernel-side counter+head/modulo idiom; a `SYS_PRIVCTL` head-carve trace would mirror `do_schedule.rs`'s `SCHED_TRACE_HEAD` block.

---

## Cross-cutting notes for implementing 4.5

- **Wiring PM into boot:** add `("minixrs-pm", workspace.join("servers/pm"), 0)` to the `servers` array in `kernel/build.rs:105-111` (change the array length `5`), and give `servers/pm/src/main.rs` a real SEF-driven body. RS (`servers/rs/src/main.rs`) and VM (`servers/vm/src/main.rs`) are the closest templates: `sef_startup(SefConfig{ init_fresh, signal_handler })` → `sef_publish_to_ds(name)` in init → `loop { sef.receive(&mut msg); match msg.m_type { … } }`. PM's `mproc` table should be a static `UnsafeCell<[…;N]>` newtype like `vm/region.rs` / `ds/registry.rs` (CLAUDE.md:144), with pure logic host-tested and `main.rs` coverage-excluded.

The signal number rides in payload `0..4`; `SefSignalCb = fn(signo: i32)` (`server-rt/src/signal.rs:10`). # minix.rs — Slice 4.5 Preparation Report (PM part A)

Crates live at the **workspace root**, not under `crates/`. The shared crate is `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/minixrs/kernel-shared/` (package `minixrs-kernel-shared`, imported as `minixrs_kernel_shared`). The kernel is `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/minixrs/kernel/`.

Key status finding up front: **PM is not yet real.** `servers/pm/src/main.rs` is a 6-line `fn main(){ /* TODO */ }`, and `kernel/build.rs:105-111` packs only VM/DS/VFS/SCHED/RS into the boot archive — PM (proc 0) is absent, so its boot slot stays blocked on `RTS_NO_PRIV`. There is **no `mproc` table, no getpid, no terminate/kill path, and `SYS_PRIVCTL` is an `ENOSYS` stub** anywhere in the tree. Slice 4.5 builds all of these.

---

## 1. `kernel-shared` — proc numbers, call numbers, message layout

### com.rs (`kernel-shared/src/com.rs`)
- Counts: `NR_TASKS=5` (:14), `NR_PROCS=1024` (:16), `NR_SYS_PROCS=64` (:18).
- Kernel tasks (negative): `ASYNCM=-5, IDLE=-4, CLOCK=-3, SYSTEM=-2, HARDWARE=-1` (:30-38).
- Servers (gen-0 boot): **`PM_PROC_NR=ProcNr::new(0)`** (:48), `VFS=1, RS=2, MEM=3, TTY=4, DS=5, MFS=6, VM=7, PFS=8, SCHED=9, INIT=10` (:49-58).
- `NR_BOOT_PROCS = INIT+1 = 11` (:62).
- **`boot_endpoint(p)`** (:69-71) = `make_endpoint(0, p)` — the const used everywhere to name a boot proc/task; PM's endpoint is `boot_endpoint(PM_PROC_NR)`.

### callnr.rs (`kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs`)
- `KERNEL_CALL = 0x600` (:11). Calls are contiguous:
  - `SYS_GETINFO=0x600` (:14), **`SYS_PRIVCTL=0x601`** (:15), `SYS_FORK=0x602`, `SYS_EXEC=0x603`, `SYS_EXIT=0x604`, `SYS_COPY, SYS_SAFECOPY, SYS_IRQCTL, SYS_VMCTL=0x608, SYS_SCHEDULE=0x609, SYS_SETALARM=0x60A, SYS_TIMES, SYS_DIAGCTL, SYS_SETGRANT, SYS_SCHEDCTL=0x60E` (:14-33).
- **`NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE4 = 15`** (:42); `NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE3` is a one-slice alias equal to it (:48). `NR_SYS_CALLS = 32` (:53) — width of `k_call_mask` (one u32 word).
- `GET_WHOAMI=12` (:70), `SYS_GETINFO_NAME_LEN=16` (:76).
- Request-range bases (all *server IPC* m_type ranges, distinct from `KERNEL_CALL`, all `< NOTIFY_MESSAGE`):
  - **VM `VM_RQ_BASE=0xC00`** (:129) — `VM_PAGEFAULT=0xC00`, `VM_BRK, VM_MMAP, VM_MUNMAP` (:135-160).
  - **SEF `SEF_RQ_BASE=0xD00`** (:233) — `SEF_INIT=0xD00`, **`SEF_SIGNAL=0xD01`** (:237-242), signal number in payload `0..4`.
  - **DS `DS_RQ_BASE=0xE00`** (:184).
  - **SCHED `SCHED_RQ_BASE=0xF00`** (:270) — `SCHEDULING_NO_QUANTUM=0xF00` (kernel-originated), `SCHEDULING_START=0xF01`, `SCHEDULING_STOP`, `SCHEDULING_SET_NICE` (:275-290). `SCHEDULING_START` doc (:277-281) explicitly says "Driven by PM/RS from slice 4.5+".
  - `SCHEDCTL_FLAG_KERNEL = 1<<0` (:38).
- `NOTIFY_MESSAGE = 0x1000` lives in `ipc_const.rs:28` (with `SEND=1, RECEIVE=2, SENDREC=3, NOTIFY=4, SENDNB=5, SENDA=16`).

### message.rs (`kernel-shared/src/message.rs`)
```rust
#[repr(C, align(8))]
pub struct Message { pub m_source: Endpoint, pub m_type: i32, pub payload: [u8; 96] } // 104 bytes
```
(:33-42; `m_source`/`m_type` at offsets 0/4, `payload` at offset 8.) **No typed accessors** — every handler reads/writes payload with `i32::from_ne_bytes(msg.payload[off..off+4])` / `u64::from_ne_bytes(...)` and `copy_from_slice(&v.to_ne_bytes())` (see the `read_i32`/`write_u64` helpers repeated in `do_schedule.rs:173-180`, `do_vmctl.rs:252-278`, `do_setalarm.rs:61-73`).

### endpoint.rs (`kernel-shared/src/endpoint.rs`)
- `Endpoint = i32` (wire format, :26); `ProcNr(i32)` (:40, `.new/.get/.is_task`), `PrivId(u16)` (:65, `.new/.get/.as_usize`), `SysId` alias of `PrivId` (:88).
- `ENDPOINT_GEN_SHIFT=15` (:92); `make_endpoint(g,p)` (:115), `endpoint_proc(e)` sign-extends so tasks come back negative (:121-130), `endpoint_gen(e)` (:133-135).
- Sentinels `ANY`, `NONE`, `SELF` all at gen 0 (:105-112).

## 2. Kernel `Proc` / `Priv` structures and statics

### `Proc` (`kernel/src/proc/proc_struct.rs:27-120`)
Fields relevant to 4.5: `nr: ProcNr`, `endpoint: Endpoint`, `priv_id: Option<PrivId>`, `rts_flags: AtomicU32` (:37), `misc_flags: u32`, `priority: u8`, `quantum_ms/quantum_left`, `user_time/sys_time`, **`alarm_at: u64`** (:57), IPC state (`caller_q/q_link: Option<ProcNr>`, `getfrom_e/sendto_e`, `send_msg/deliver_msg/deliver_msg_vir`), `ttbr0_pa/asid`, `page_fault_state/heap_window`, **`scheduler: Endpoint`** (:110, `NONE`=kernel-scheduled), `next_ready: Option<ProcNr>`, `name: [u8;16]`.
`Proc::EMPTY` (:127-162) initializes `rts_flags = RTS_SLOT_FREE`, `endpoint = NONE`, `priv_id = None`, `scheduler = NONE`.

### `Priv` (`kernel/src/proc/priv_struct.rs:51-84`)
```rust

    pub id: PrivId, pub proc_nr: Option<ProcNr>, pub flags: u16, pub trap_mask: u16,
    pub ipc_to: [u32; 2], pub k_call_mask: [u32; 1],
    pub notify_pending: [u32; 2], pub asyn_pending: [u32; 2],
    pub sig_mgr: Endpoint,                      // :72 — signal manager endpoint
    pub io_ranges/mem_ranges/irqs: ArrayVec<…>, pub grant_table: u64, pub grant_entries: u32,
```
